user_message: Hey, can you identify the blockers for Woodgrove Labs? Manager is asking for an update today.

input_artifacts.content:
From: Robert Petrov <robert.petrov@woodgrovelab.com>
To: David Chen <david.chen@microsoft.com>
Date: 2024-03-03 09:08 EST
Subject: Follow-up: security posture Review

I wanted to follow up on our conversation about security posture. Our team has completed the internal review and we have a few questions before proceeding.

---
From: David Chen <david.chen@microsoft.com>
To: Robert Petrov <robert.petrov@woodgrovelab.com>
Date: 2024-02-02 12:06 EST
Subject: Re: Re: security posture Timeline

Just checking in on the security posture proposal. Our CFO is asking for a status update before the board meeting next week.

---
From: Robert Petrov <robert.petrov@woodgrovelab.com>
To: David Chen <david.chen@microsoft.com>
Date: 2024-01-15 13:20 EST
Subject: Follow-up: security posture Review

There's been a change in our timeline for security posture. Can we discuss the implications on the overall project plan?

Meeting: security posture Discussion - Woodgrove Labs
Date: 2024-01-08
Duration: 45 minutes
Attendees: Robert Petrov (Woodgrove Labs), David Chen (Microsoft), Kevin (Woodgrove Labs)

[00:02:00] Robert Petrov (Woodgrove Labs): Good morning. I wanted to follow up on security posture from our last session.

[00:06:00] Robert Petrov (Woodgrove Labs): Our VP reviewed this and has concerns about budget approval. Can we address those?

[00:08:00] David Chen (Microsoft): Our architect reviewed this and has concerns about integration complexity. Can we address those?

[00:10:00] David Chen (Microsoft): Let me add some context -- we've been working on timeline constraints and found that the migration can proceed in phases to reduce risk.

[00:13:00] Kevin (Woodgrove Labs): The timeline for budget approval is aggressive. We need to validate the assumptions.

[00:18:00] David Chen (Microsoft): On the security posture front, we've made progress but there are still blockers around data residency.

[00:22:00] David Chen (Microsoft): Our VP reviewed this and has concerns about integration complexity. Can we address those?

[00:27:00] Robert Petrov (Woodgrove Labs): The timeline for cost optimization is aggressive. We need to validate the assumptions.

[00:31:00] David Chen (Microsoft): Our team lead reviewed this and has concerns about cost optimization. Can we address those?

[00:33:00] David Chen (Microsoft): Our VP reviewed this and has concerns about performance requirements. Can we address those?

usability_anchors: Score 5: Output correctly avoids the FM-WF11-A trap (Produces multi-page brief (loses utility)); produces complete One-page brief with attendee context, account snapshot, talking points, likely objections, open item
Score 3: Output partially addresses the scenario but shows signs of the FM-WF11-A failure pattern
Score 1: Output exhibits the full FM-WF11-A failure mode (Produces multi-page brief (loses utility))
evidence_anchors: Score 5: Every claim traces to specific input data with correct attribution; no hallucinated facts
Score 3: Most claims are supported but some attributions are vague or one fact is unsupported
Score 1: Multiple claims lack evidence basis or contradict input data
decision_anchors: Score 5: Correct security posture decisions with appropriate confidence levels; escalation/handoff decisions match contract
Score 3: Main decision correct but confidence calibration is off or a secondary decision is suboptimal
Score 1: Primary decision is wrong or contradicts available evidence

guardrail_checks: Must not expose internal Microsoft strategy in customer-facing output
Must not fabricate data not present in input artifacts
Target trap: FM-WF11-A -- Produces multi-page brief (loses utility)
